Q1: My study routine was honestly kind of chaotic, but I tried to make it work around my limited resources. I'd usually set aside time in the evenings after my other responsibilities, listening to recorded lectures while doing household tasks or during my commute. I aimed for about four hours a day across the week, which added up, but it wasn't always quality time. The anxiety made it hard to stick to a perfect schedule, so I'd often end up cramming more hours when I felt behind.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and how overwhelmed I was feeling. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, logging in regularly and keeping up. But then stress would hit, or something in my personal life would demand attention, and suddenly I'd be scrambling to catch up before deadlines. I tried to have regular times, but life with limited resources doesn't always cooperate with ideal study schedules.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline approaching or seeing a notification about new content that got me to log in. Sometimes my peer support group would message saying we should check something together, which was actually really helpful motivation. Other times it was pure anxiety—this nagging feeling that I was falling behind and needed to at least see what was happening in the course. The social aspect with my peers became a big driver honestly.
Q4: The peer support intervention actually helped make logging in feel more normal for me. Once I connected with a few classmates who were also struggling, we'd remind each other to check the LMS and discuss things. It became less of a chore and more of a social thing. Before that, logging in felt isolating and stressful, but having people to talk to about the content changed that. It still wasn't perfectly routine, but it was better.
Q5: The recorded lectures were absolutely essential for me since I learn best by listening. I could replay sections I didn't understand, pause to take notes, and listen while doing other things. The discussion forums were also valuable because interacting with peers helped concepts stick better than just reading alone. When someone explained something in their own words, it often clicked for me in a way the textbook didn't.
Q6: I mostly ignored the supplementary reading materials and some of the optional quizzes. With limited resources, I couldn't always access everything easily, and I had to prioritize what would directly impact my grade. The dense PDF readings felt overwhelming when I was already anxious, so I'd tell myself I'd get to them later and then never did. Time was precious and I had to make hard choices about what to engage with.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the exams or assignments, or if it helped reduce my anxiety about the material. Anything that made concepts clearer through audio or discussion was valuable to me. I also valued activities that my peer group was doing together because that social accountability made the effort feel more meaningful. If something felt like busywork without clear purpose, I couldn't justify spending my limited energy on it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford, honestly. Required ones had this weight of necessity—I had to do them regardless of how I was feeling. Optional ones would sit there making me feel guilty because I knew they'd probably help, but when you're already stretched thin and anxious, 'optional' becomes 'skip.' There was this constant tension between wanting to do everything and accepting I just couldn't.
Q9: Oh absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download the lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone when I didn't have reliable internet. I also recorded myself summarizing concepts and played those back, which is kind of embarrassing to admit but it really helped me learn. I borrowed textbooks from classmates and took handwritten notes that I'd review away from any screen. The LMS was just one piece of my studying.
Q10: There were many times I'd be studying intensely but the LMS wouldn't show it at all. Like when I'd listen to a downloaded lecture three or four times while commuting—that's hours of engagement that looks like nothing online. Or when my peer group would meet in person to discuss concepts and quiz each other. I'd also spend time just thinking through problems in my head, working through the logic without being connected to anything trackable.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd log in, download what I needed, and then work with those materials offline for days. Internet access isn't always reliable for me, so I learned to grab everything in one session and then study independently. A single short login might represent a whole week's worth of actual engagement with those materials. It was more efficient for my situation even if it looked like I wasn't doing much.
Q12: There were definitely periods where my anxiety spiked and engaging felt almost impossible. Around midterms, I remember feeling so overwhelmed that even opening the LMS made my heart race. Financial stress was also a factor—when you're worried about basic needs, focusing on coursework becomes incredibly difficult. The peer support helped pull me through those moments, but there were dark weeks where I just couldn't show up the way I wanted to.
Q13: All of those things affected me significantly. The workload felt crushing sometimes, especially when multiple courses had overlapping deadlines. Fatigue was constant because I was balancing so many responsibilities outside of school. When I was confused about material, my anxiety would spike and I'd sometimes avoid the LMS entirely rather than face feeling stupid. My competing responsibilities meant I couldn't always engage when I wanted to, only when I absolutely had to.
Q14: Definitely. There were times I'd be scrolling through the LMS, technically 'active,' but my mind was somewhere else entirely. Anxiety does this thing where you're physically present but mentally checked out. I'd watch videos but realize I'd absorbed nothing and have to rewatch later. Sometimes I was just going through the motions to feel like I was doing something, even when I knew I wasn't really learning anything meaningful.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—like this huge wall I couldn't climb. I'd tell myself I'd do it when I felt better, but that better feeling rarely came on its own. The peer support actually helped with this because having someone to work alongside made starting less terrifying. But yes, avoidance was a real pattern for me, driven by fear of not being good enough.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage. I'd have periods of low activity and then intense bursts right before something was due. It wasn't ideal and it stressed me out, but that pressure was often the only thing that could override my anxiety and get me moving. I'd log in frantically, submit things, and then retreat again. The deadline was like an external force that temporarily overpowered my internal resistance.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback gave me a temporary boost and made me want to engage more. But critical feedback, even when constructive, could send my anxiety spiraling and make me avoid the course for days. I know that's not rational, but with low resilience, every critique felt personal. The peer support helped me process feedback better because we'd discuss it together and normalize struggling.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and challenging. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me something concrete to work toward, which I needed. But the pace sometimes felt relentless, and when I fell behind, catching up felt impossible. I wished there was more flexibility built in for students like me who have unpredictable life circumstances. The structure assumed a stability I didn't always have.
Q19: The availability of recorded lectures made engagement much easier for me as an auditory learner. The discussion forums were also accessible and didn't require being online at specific times. What made it harder was when activities required consistent internet access or assumed everyone had the same resources. Some of the interactive elements didn't work well on my older devices, which was frustrating and made me feel excluded from parts of the course.
Q20: I think that pattern could show someone who's trying hard but struggling to produce finished work. Maybe they're anxious about submitting imperfect assignments, or they're dealing with external pressures that interrupt their workflow. High activity might mean they're engaging, learning, participating in discussions, but something is blocking that final step of submission. It could also be confusion about what's actually required or perfectionism getting in the way.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. I performed okay on exams despite not always showing high LMS activity because a lot of my learning happened offline. Someone might understand the material well from other sources—textbooks, study groups, prior knowledge—and not need to engage with every online resource. The LMS isn't the only place learning happens, and some students are just more efficient or have different learning styles that don't require constant platform use.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns of when you're active, what you click on, and submission times. They capture the digital footprint pretty well. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, the emotional state behind the activity, and all the learning that happens offline. They can't see me listening to recordings on the bus, discussing concepts with peers over coffee, or lying awake at night mentally reviewing material. The log sees the shadow, not the substance.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely connecting with the material and caring about understanding it, not just going through motions. For me, it's when I'm actively thinking, questioning, and trying to relate concepts to things I already know. It's also about being part of a learning community—talking with peers, contributing to discussions, feeling like I belong. True engagement is mental and emotional investment, which doesn't always translate to clicks and timestamps.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how much the peer support intervention changed everything for me. It wouldn't capture the anxiety attacks before logging in, or the relief when a classmate helped me understand something. It can't see that I'm a 25-year-old with limited resources trying my hardest despite everything working against me. The data misses the human story—the stress, the small victories, the times I almost gave up but didn't. That's the real engagement that matters.
